channel.title: Sweet-Sour on zhouyang — The 25th Frame
channel.description: Recent content in Sweet-Sour on zhouyang — The 25th Frame

item.title: Guo Bao Rou — The Double-Fried Pork That Snaps When You Bite
item.description: The first thing that hits you is the vinegar. Not the polite, subtle kind — the sharp, sweet-tart smell of aged mǐ cù hitting a hot wok, caramelizing on contact, billowing through the kitchen door like someone opened a window onto a different season. You’re sitting in a Dongbei restaurant in Shanghai in January, the kind where the windows fog up from the steam of boiling dumplings and the tables are covered in plastic sheeting. The heater rattles. Outside it’s three degrees. Inside, the plate that just landed on your table is still crackling.

item.title: How to Cook Guo Bao Rou: The Double-Fry Way
item.description:
Editor’s Note: When we published the story of Guo Bao Rou, several readers asked the same question: how do you actually get that shatter at home? The double-fry technique looks intimidating but it’s really just two temperatures and a clock. Here’s the step-by-step.

If you’ve ever bitten into Guo Bao Rou that went crack — the kind where your table neighbors turned to look — you know the standard that matters. The shatter is everything. This recipe gives you that shatter, in a home kitchen, with a wok and a thermometer. It takes about thirty minutes. It will ruin takeout fried pork for you permanently.

item.title: How to Cook Sweet and Sour Pork: The Real Cantonese Way
item.description:
Editor’s Note: Since launching this column, we’ve heard one request more than any other: the stories are great, but how do we actually cook these? Today we’re fixing that — six classic recipes, dropping at once. Here’s how to make 糖醋里脊（咕噜肉）的正宗做法。

This is the practical companion to Sweet and Sour Pork: The Cantonese Classic the World Keeps Getting Wrong — read that first for the history, then come back here to cook.

item.title: Sweet and Sour Pork: The Cantonese Classic the World Keeps Getting Wrong
item.description: The wok. The oil hits 190°C and the kitchen fills with a specific sound — a violent sizzle that collapses into a rolling crackle. You’re in a restaurant on Huifu East Road in Guangzhou’s Yuexiu district, and the smell coming off that wok is white vinegar and rock sugar caramelizing together, sharp and sweet and completely unavoidable. The chef works fast: pork out, drain, back in. Second fry, hotter. The batter turns from pale gold to deep amber. He tips in the sauce and everything roars. Bell pepper chunks, pineapple wedges, onion petals tumble through the orange-red gloss. Ten seconds from sauce to plate.
